dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 6 for childbirth at 16th and Broad.
Hi, I'm Dan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I'm having really strong contractions and my water just broke!
Can you tell me your name and where you are?
I see you are blinking without stimulation. That's a sign of alertness.
What is your name?
Can you squeeze my hand? I see you responding well. Great job.
Are you having any trouble breathing right now?
Let's take your respiratory rate quickly, okay?
She has an even breathing pattern at 20 breaths per minute.
Your skin feels warm and dry.
Fast pulse at around 128 beats per minute.
Normal skin tone with good turgor.
Blood pressure is 117 over 75.
I'll place a pulse oximeter now. That's a sensor for your finger.
We're going to hook you up to an EKG machine to check your heart rhythm.
Have you been pregnant before, and how many babies have you had?
I've been pregnant four times with two live births and one miscarriage.
When did your water break? Are the contractions regular?
My water broke about five minutes ago. I've been contracting for over an hour now.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how bad would you say this pain is?
It's a 10. I can't get comfortable anywhere.
Are the contractions coming regularly or randomly?
Every one or two minutes right now. It's nonstop.
Your skin feels fine with no signs of injury.
We are repeating the pulse at 111 beats a minute now.
The breathing rate remains at 20.
Blood pressure is now 114 over 71.
Oxygen is at 99% now.
The elevated heart rate is still sinus tachycardia, nothing concerning.
I'm placing the 4 lead EKG monitor for closer heart observation.
Let's secure an IV in case we need to give fluids or medicine later.
I'm giving you a small bolus of normal saline for IV support.
We're staying with the OB/GYN protocol for delivery.
I note symmetrical chest rise and bilateral full expansion.
I observed crowning of the head and mucus with fluid discharging from the vaginal area.
I need to retake vitals before changes.
Your pulse is now down to 99, blood pressure is 123 over 75, and your respiratory rate is now 18.
Unfortunately, I can't obtain a glucose result in the field at this time.
IV continues to run normally.
Where would you prefer we take you both, the baby and you?
We need to slowly secure you on the stretcher after delivery starts.
You will move with our help. We will support your back during contractions.
I see you pushing-it's nearly time. You feel like you need to push really hard.
Let's document the final vital signs before the baby is delivered.
We are preparing the baby for the nursery nurse and team. They'll take care of your baby for the next few hours.
We'll take the baby into the warmer and cover with a towel for warmth.
We are calling into Chippenham now to inform them of the delivery.
You handled the move very well. Great job.
Just noted some burning sensation you mentioned-we don't see anything visible.
Vaginal bleeding appears to be controlled at this time.
IV is flowing normally with no complications.
Oxygen drops to 0. We will monitor again in a short time.
I see no rashes or injuries but note PMS x 4 in your extremities.
How would you rate the pain now?
Still a 10, nothing has changed.
The baby has an Apgar score of 7, based on appearance, pulse, grimace, activity, and respiration.
I don't see any visible rashes.
We'll make sure the IV continues while the baby is being handled carefully.
You and the baby are both secured and ready for transport.
You can keep holding the baby briefly before we transfer you both.
Let's call in the condition of you and the baby to the hospital now.
One more round of vitals to ensure everything's stable during transport.
Thank you for your cooperation. It's going to be smooth from here.
Got it all packed and secured. We're leaving now for the hospital.
Baby is wrapped in a towel and in the warmer, and ready for transport with the warmest care.
Everything here is ready, mother and baby are secured, and the call is signed in with the hospital.
Final note: G4 P2, alert and oriented with a GCS of 15. No meds or allergies.
No injuries noted on you. We just need to ensure the baby is handled with care.
The IV line is secure, and we have some backup just in case.
Walk slowly and step safely as we move you toward the door. You got this.
All set to transfer you and the baby. I'm carrying the baby, so don't worry.
The IV has been started at 20 gauge in your left hand, and the line is going well.
You moved onto the stretcher without any incident. Great job.
Your oxygen is 0%, but you're not showing signs of distress.
You transferred to the stretcher on your own. We're tracking you well.
I still don't see redness or rashes on your skin.
The IV worked on your first try. Great job by the EMS team.
Keep pushing as needed. Your contractions are getting you close to the end.
We are securing the baby now so you can both be transferred together safely.
Your due date was on the 10th, and you delivered early. We'll note that on the EPCR.
The delivery goes smoothly but is early. Our focus now is on getting you and the baby to the hospital safely.
I just want to thank you for your strength and calm during this time.
We will keep monitoring you and the baby closely for any changes.
Let's keep this on track and bring you and the baby in for a smooth handover.
You did a fantastic job on your own, especially with such strong pushing.
We will keep monitoring you and the baby closely for any changes.
Holding up well-keep going, you're almost there.
We passed you off to the hospital with baby and EPCR successfully following the Childbirth protocol.
Final care summary is complete and given to the nurse. Pathing complete for the mother and child.
Vaginal bleeding remained controlled by placing a 5x9 gauze inside the vaginal region.
The IV has been running well and will continue during transport to prevent dehydration.
You're doing great under pressure. Keep pushing.
Let's carefully wheel you to the ambulance. All stretcher restraints are secure.
The baby looks strong and healthy from the first assessment. You did great.
I see the hospital is aware of our arrival and has the nursery team on standby.
Let's complete the final oxygen reading before transport to ensure it's up to level.
There are no visible signs of distress in either of you or the baby.
The 4-lead EKG is still stable and no arrhythmia has been observed.
Let's get the baby into the warmer and keep him at consistent temperature.
